title: Inner Self vs Outward Senses
summary: How the Creator made the senses turn outward, but the wise man turns them inward to see the Soul. Addresses sensory distractions, focus issues, and screen addiction.

sections.summary: Themes: action and duty, self-knowledge, devotion. The passage moves from “The Self-Created (God) made the senses turn outward; hence one looks outward, does not see what is within oneself. Rare is the wise man, who,…” through “Like the child well guarded in the womb by the pregnant women, the all-knowing Agni, who lies hidden in the fire-sticks, is worshipped every day…” through “As water that is pure, when poured into water that is pure remains pure, so does the Self of the realised one remains pure on knowing (uniting…”

attribution.rights: Public domain in the United States

verses.sanskrit: पराञ्चि खानि व्यतृणत् स्वयम्भू- स्तस्मात्पराङ्पश्यति नान्तरात्मन् । कश्चिद्धीरः प्रत्यगात्मानमैक्ष- दावृत्तचक्षुरमृतत्वमिच्छन् ॥
verses.translation: The Self-Created (God) made the senses turn outward; hence one looks outward, does not see what is within oneself. Rare is the wise man, who, longing for immortality, turns his eyes inward and sees the inner Atman.

verses.sanskrit: पराचः कामाननुयन्ति बाला- स्ते मृत्योर्यन्ति विततस्य पाशम् । अथ धीरा अमृतत्वं विदित्वा ध्रुवमध्रुवेष्विह न प्रार्थयन्ते ॥
verses.translation: The ignorant pursue outward pleasures and walk into the wide-spread net of death. The wise, however, recognising the eternal , among the things that are fleeting , do not seek anything .

verses.sanskrit: येन रूपं रसं गन्धं शब्दान् स्पर्शाꣳश्च मैथुनान् । एतेनैव विजानाति किमत्र परिशिष्यते । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: That by which man sees, tastes, smells, hears and enjoys, is the omniscient Lord. By that alone man has knowledge. What is there that remains unknown to It? This is indeed That (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: स्वप्नान्तं जागरितान्तं चोभौ येनानुपश्यति । महान्तं विभुमात्मानं मत्वा धीरो न शोचति ॥
verses.translation: That by which one perceives things both in dream and in the waking state - having known that as the great omni-present Self, the wise man does not grieve.

verses.sanskrit: य इमं मध्वदं वेद आत्मानं जीवमन्तिकात् । ईशानं भूतभव्यस्य न ततो विजुगुप्सते । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: He who knows the Atman, the enjoyer of the fruits of action, sustainer of life, as the Lord of the past and future, as close at hand -- he fears no more. This is indeed That (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: यः पूर्वं तपसो जातमद्भ्यः पूर्वमजायत । गुहां प्रविश्य तिष्ठन्तं यो भूतेभिर्व्यपश्यत । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: He who sees the First-Born - born of knowledge, born before the creation of waters - sees Him within the heart, dwelling among the elements , sees Brahman indeed. This is verily That (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: या प्राणेन संभवत्यदितिर्देवतामयी । गुहां प्रविश्य तिष्ठन्तीं या भूतेभिर्व्यजायत । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: Aditi, the power of all powers, manifested in the form of Prana, embodied in the elements, who resides in the heart, is the Immortal Self. This is truly That (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: अरण्योर्निहितो जातवेदा गर्भ इव सुभृतो गर्भिणीभिः । दिवे दिवे ईड्यो जागृवद्भिर्हविष्मद्भिर्मनुष्येभिरग्निः । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: Like the child well guarded in the womb by the pregnant women, the all-knowing Agni, who lies hidden in the fire-sticks, is worshipped every day by the awakened man and by those who offer oblations in the Fire. This is verily that (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: यतश्चोदेति सूर्योऽस्तं यत्र च गच्छति । तं देवाः सर्वेऽर्पितास्तदु नात्येति कश्चन । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: The Atman is really that from which the sun rises and into which it mixes again. To That (Atman) are all the Gods attached and no one can actually go beyond it (Self or Atman that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: यदेवेह तदमुत्र यदमुत्र तदन्विह । मृत्योः स मृत्युमाप्नोति य इह नानेव पश्यति ॥
verses.translation: Whatever is here is also there; whatever is there is the same here. He who sees a difference here travels from death to death.

verses.sanskrit: मनसैवेदमाप्तव्यं नेह नानाऽस्ति किंचन । मृत्योः स मृत्युं गच्छति य इह नानेव पश्यति ॥
verses.translation: By the purified mind alone is This to be realised. There is no multiplicity here. From death to death he goes , who sees as if there is difference here.

verses.sanskrit: अङ्गुष्ठमात्रः पुरुषो मध्य आत्मनि तिष्ठति । ईशानं भूतभव्यस्य न ततो विजुगुप्सते । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: The Purusha of the size of a thumb dwells within the body. He is the lord of the past and future. After knowing Him one fears no more. This is verily That (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: अङ्गुष्ठमात्रः पुरुषो ज्योतिरिवाधूमकः । ईशानो भूतभव्यस्य स एवाद्य स उ श्वः । एतद्वै तत् ॥
verses.translation: The thumbsized Purusha , the lord of the past and the future, is like a flame without smoke; He stays the same today and tomorrow. This is verily That. (Self that Nachiketa was asking about).

verses.sanskrit: यथोदकं दुर्गे वृष्टं पर्वतेषु विधावति । एवं धर्मान् पृथक् पश्यंस्तानेवानुविधावति ॥
verses.translation: As rain, fallen on a hill, streams down its sides, so runs he after many births, who sees manifoldness in the Self.

verses.sanskrit: यथोदकं शुद्धे शुद्धमासिक्तं तादृगेव भवति । एवं मुनेर्विजानत आत्मा भवति गौतम ॥
verses.translation: As water that is pure, when poured into water that is pure remains pure, so does the Self of the realised one remains pure on knowing (uniting with) Brahman , O! Gautama !
